assessment.body:
## Verdict

agent_supports: yes
daemon_capability: full; driver_capability: ready (driver gap was closed by d2bf47d9)
applicable: true — recorded 2026-05-28 (events.jsonl: two distinct UUID rollout sessions, two distinct PIDs, three independent working→ready arcs across the two sessions). Spec passes 6/6 phases.

## Reasoning

`multiple-sessions-same-cwd` (scenario-meanings.md): two instances of the SAME agent launched in one working directory must appear as two distinct session rows, each with its own session_id and PID, transitioning state independently with no flapping and no PID cross-contamination. Primitive exercised = PID-based session isolation. Distinct from `session-resume` (one session_id reused across PID lifetimes) and from `multiple-agents-same-workspace` (different agent types).

**agent_supports: yes.** Pi launches as an independent REPL per invocation; two `pi` processes can run in the same cwd as-shipped. Each mints its own session UUID and writes a distinct transcript at `~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/<ts>_<uuid>.jsonl` — the path is keyed by cwd-slug + timestamp + UUID, so two sessions in one cwd produce two distinct transcript files (capabilities.json: session_id_assignment:false — pi mints its own UUID, so a recipe cannot pre-pin the two ids).

**daemon_capability: full (live observation).** Source = FilesUnderRoot{Dir: ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, Parser: JSONLineParser{NewParser: () => &Parser{}}} (agent.go). The fswatcher picks up two new transcript files (one per UUID) → two SessionState rows, and the JSONL tailer instantiates a FRESH stateful Parser{} per file, so the two sessions never share parser state — a turn on one transcript only updates that session's metrics. The recording confirms independent working→ready arcs on both UUIDs: s1 shows multiple arcs (turn 1 lazy-batched, turn 2, post-start_session re-drive turn 3); s2 shows its own arc.

**PID binding (live observation overrode the pessimistic prior).** Prior assessment predicted that pi's DiscoverPID = DiscoverPIDByCWD (`pi/pid.go` → `processlifecycle.DiscoverPIDByCWD("pi", cwd, disambiguate)`) would fail to deterministically bind each same-cwd session to its own PID, because both pi PIDs survive the cwd narrowing and the daemon would have to pick between them. The live recording showed this concern did NOT manifest for the typical sequential-launch flow: events.jsonl has exactly one pid_discovered per UUID session (s1→PID 56032 at +0:31s, s2→PID 56852 at +1:25s), no duplicates, no re-discovery, no cross-row bindings. The reason: once session 1's PID is bound (while it's still the only pi in the candidate set), the binding is sticky and never re-resolved; when session 2 is then born, the candidate set grows to two but session 1 keeps its existing binding, and session 2 falls back to the disambiguate callback whose default 'highest PID' happens to be session 2's genuinely-newer PID. The algorithm IS still structurally weaker than codex (DiscoverPIDByTranscriptWriter — binds by which process holds each rollout file open) and claudecode (metadata-first ~/.claude/sessions/<pid>.json lookup + strict unambiguous-or-zero cwd fallback hardened in #109/#169) — concurrent launches (truly simultaneous spawn) or PID reuse could still produce mis-bindings — but those failure modes are not exercised by the sequential-launch shape this scenario records. Verdict: daemon_capability:full, with the structural caveat documented (not asserted in the spec).

## Why this cell is now applicable:true

Commit d2bf47d9 extended drive-pi-interactive.sh with the start_session step type, inline {"session":N} per-step session-target routing, and the bare session focus-switch arm — closing the driver_gap that previously held this cell at applicable:false. Recording follows the intended arc: session 1 turn 1 + turn 2 → start_session (spawns concurrent session 2 in the SAME cwd) → session 2 turn 1 + turn 2 → session 1 re-drive (turn 3 on its original UUID, proving it stayed observable as a distinct row while session 2 was alive). The driver's start_session implementation pins the active slot's transcript before spawning the new slot (resolve_transcript on the prior slot, then alloc_slot for the new one), which avoids the new slot's `find -newer` poll binding to the OLD <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl when the prior turn is still streaming.

## Status of this cell

Verdict: agent_supports yes, daemon_capability full, driver_capability ready (confidence 0.86). Recording committed 2026-05-28 with 6/6 spec phases passing. The structural PID-binding weakness is documented in caveats but deliberately not asserted by the spec — hardening pi's DiscoverPID to per-transcript-writer or per-PID-metadata-file is a separate maintainer task (file an issue only if a concurrent-launch failure mode is actually observed in production).
caveats: DRIVER GAP CLOSED (was: applicable:false). The pi interactive driver was extended in commit d2bf47d9 with start_session + inline {"session":N} per-step routing + bare session step; driver_capability is now ready. Recipe + recording committed.

PID-BINDING SEAM (live observability finding): the prior pessimistic prediction — 'two pi processes in the SAME cwd both survive cwd narrowing, so the daemon cannot deterministically bind each session to its own PID' — was NOT borne out by the live recording. The recording's events.jsonl shows two distinct pid_discovered events, one per UUID rollout session, each pinned to its own distinct PID (s1→PID 56032, s2→PID 56852), with no flapping, duplicate cross-row bindings, or re-discovery once bound. The algorithm IS structurally weaker than codex's per-rollout-file-writer binding and claudecode's metadata-first lookup, but its sticky-once-bound + highest-PID disambiguate fallback happens to resolve sequential same-cwd launches correctly: session 1's PID is bound while it's alone in the candidate set, then session 2's launch adds a new PID and the fallback's 'highest PID' is genuinely the new one. The algorithm remains vulnerable to concurrent (truly simultaneous) launches and to PID reuse, but those are not exercised by this scenario. daemon_capability stays full; the spec deliberately does NOT assert stable per-PID binding, so the algorithmic weakness is documented as a caveat rather than a recording-failing invariant.

PHASE ANCHORING (live confirmation): pi's process scanner (ExactName{pi}, pgrep -x) does emit a transient proc-<PID> presession row born in ready before the UUID transcript is discovered — confirmed in events.jsonl (proc-56032, proc-56852). Every UUID-keyed phase in expected.jsonl must therefore anchor on a working transition, never on ready, so the matcher skips the proc-<PID> placeholder. The live recording also showed pi's lazy-materialization behavior is timing-variable: in this run, turn 1 DID emit a (microsecond-fast, 14ms) working→ready arc — unlike pi/checkpoint-rewind where turn 1 was fully batched. The recipe sends two prompts per session for robustness, and the spec's working anchor matches the first observable arc regardless of how pi batches turn 1.

pi mints its own session UUID (session_id_assignment:false), so — like codex and unlike claudecode — a recipe cannot pre-pin the two ids; the spec asserts two DISTINCT sessions via new_session/same_session_as relations.

sources.note: multiple-sessions-same-cwd: two instances of the same agent in one cwd → two distinct session_id+PID rows, independent transitions, stable non-cross-contaminating PID binding; primitive = PID-based session isolation; distinct from session-resume and multiple-agents-same-workspace.

DRIVER GAP root cause: single-session driver. case labels = send|slash|wait_turn|interrupt|sleep only; one tmux SESSION, one TRANSCRIPT, one EXPECTED_TURNS counter; no start_session, no {"session":N} routing. Cannot stage two concurrent same-cwd pi sessions.

Contrast: the codex driver HAS step_start_session + slot allocation + per-step {"session":N} routing with claimed-path exclusion. This is the concurrent-second-session primitive the pi driver lacks; the codex/claudecode recipes for this scenario depend on it.

Source: FilesUnderRoot{Dir: ~/.pi/agent/sessions/, Parser: JSONLineParser{NewParser: () => &Parser{}}} — a fresh stateful parser per transcript file means two pi sessions in one cwd do not share parser state (independent arcs are observable). Process.Match = ExactName{pi} (pgrep -x).

DiscoverPID delegates to processlifecycle.DiscoverPIDByCWD("pi", cwd, disambiguate): cwd-only narrowing. Weaker than codex's per-rollout-file-writer binding and claudecode's metadata-first lookup; two same-cwd pi processes both survive the narrowing → cannot be cleanly bound to distinct PIDs by cwd alone (irrlicht_observes:partial).

DiscoverPIDByCWD → narrowByCWD: lists matching PIDs, filters by cwd, and on multiple matches falls back to disambiguate (default highest PID). No per-process metadata and no per-file-writer probe, so two same-cwd processes are indistinguishable by this path alone.
headless_mode:true (scenario requires it); session_id_assignment:false (pi mints its own UUID — recipe cannot pre-pin the two ids); subagents:false, permission_hooks:false.

Sibling codex cell: recorded (yes/yes) via the codex driver's start_session model. Same spec shape; the pi spec is adapted from it. The pi cell is blocked only by the pi driver lacking that step model.

preconditions: pi CLI on PATH and authenticated (pi runs against its configured model out-of-band)
tmux available — the interactive driver launches each pi REPL in its own tmux session
irrlichd is recording — run-cell.sh isolated mode (spawns its own --record daemon), or --attach against a daemon started with --record
no other pi session is live in the run cwd (a stray same-cwd pi would surface as a third row and break the two-row assertion)
setup: Driver auto-starts session 1 (slot 1) as a pi REPL in <staging>/cwd; the first send lazy-materializes session 1's <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl transcript under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/<cwd-slug>/

The start_session step launches session 2 (slot 2) in the SAME <staging>/cwd in a fresh tmux/process — its first send lazy-materializes a SECOND distinct <ts>_<uuid>.jsonl in the same project dir (pi keys transcripts by UUID, not by cwd)
Steps carrying {"session":1} route send/wait_turn back to session 1 while session 2 stays alive — exercising the concurrent, independent arcs across two distinct UUID rollouts

LAZY-MATERIALIZATION (discovered live in pi/checkpoint-rewind): pi creates each per-session JSONL only AFTER the first user message commits, with turn 1 batched into that initial write; turn 1's working→ready arc is therefore NOT daemon-observable. The recipe sends a SECOND prompt per session so the spec can anchor identity on an observable working transition

PID-BINDING SEAM (caveat, not asserted): pi's DiscoverPID = DiscoverPIDByCWD (cwd-only narrowing + highest-PID disambiguate fallback) — two pi processes sharing one cwd both survive the narrowing, so the daemon may flap or mis-bind PIDs; the spec asserts what IS observable (two distinct session_id rows, each with its own birth + working→ready arc) and leaves PID-binding stability documented as a known weakness

script.text: Reply with exactly the word: alpha
Reply with exactly the word: alpha2
Reply with exactly the word: bravo
Reply with exactly the word: bravo2
Reply with exactly the word: alpha3

verify: events.jsonl contains 2 distinct pi session_ids (UUID-keyed rollouts), both keyed to the same cwd slug under ~/.pi/agent/sessions/
each session_id has at least one independent working->ready arc; no working transition on one session is attributed to the other

session 1 has MULTIPLE working->ready arcs (at least one pre-start_session + at least one post-start_session re-drive); session 2 has at least one — proving session 1 stayed observable as a distinct row while session 2 was alive

every UUID-keyed phase anchors on a WORKING transition (never on ready), to skip pi's transient proc-<PID> presession placeholder (born in ready, never enters working) and pi's variable lazy-materialization behavior (turn 1 sometimes emits a microsecond-fast batched arc, sometimes doesn't)

PID-binding stability is NOT asserted: pi's cwd-only DiscoverPID is structurally vulnerable to same-cwd ambiguity, though in practice the sticky-once-bound + highest-PID fallback resolves sequential launches correctly (this recording confirmed two distinct PIDs were bound to the two UUID sessions without flapping — see assessment.json for the live observability correction)
